title: FTSO: EFFECTIVE NAS VIA FIRST TOPOLOGY SECOND OPERATOR [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## FTSO: EFFECTIVE NAS VIA FIRST TOPOLOGY SECOND OPERATOR

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Existing one-shot neural architecture search (NAS) methods generally contain a giant super-net, which leads to heavy computational cost. Our method, named FTSO, separates the whole architecture search into two sub-steps. In the first step, we only search for the topology, and in the second step, we only search for the operators. FTSO not only reduces NAS’s search time from days to 0.68 seconds, but also significantly improves the accuracy. Specifically, our experiments on ImageNet show that within merely 18 seconds, FTSO can achieve 76.4% testing accuracy, 1.5% higher than the baseline, PC-DARTS. In addition, FTSO can reach 97.77% testing accuracy, 0.27% higher than the baseline, with 99.8% of search time saved on CIFAR10.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Since the great success of AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) in image classification, most modern machine learning models have been developed based on deep neural networks. For neural networks, their performance is greatly determined by the architectures. Thus, in the past decade, a tremendous amount of work (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015; Szegedy et al., 2015; He et al., 2016) has been done to investigate proper network architecture design. However, as the network size has grown larger and larger, it has gradually become unaffordable to manually search for better network architectures via trial and error due to the expensive time and resource overhead. To ease this problem, a new technique called neural architecture search (NAS) was introduced, which allows computers to search for better network architectures automatically instead of relying on human experts..

Early-proposed reinforcement learning-based NAS methods (Zoph & Le, 2017; Baker et al., 2017; Zoph et al., 2018) typically have an RNN-based controller to sample candidate network architectures from the search space. Although these algorithms can provide promising accuracy, their computation cost is usually unaffordable, e.g., 1800 GPU-days are required for NASNet.

To ease the search efficiency problem, one-shot approaches (Pham et al., 2018; Cai et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019) with parameter sharing were proposed. These methods first create a huge directed acyclic graph (DAG) super-net, containing the whole search space. Then, the kernel weights are shared among all the sampled architectures via the super-net. This strategy makes it possible to measure the candidate architecture’s performance without repeatedly retraining it from scratch. However, these algorithms suffer from the super-nets’ computational overheads. This problem is particularly severe for differentiable models (Liu et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020).

Limited by current NAS algorithms’ inefficiency, it is rather challenging to find satisfying network architectures on large-scale datasets and high-level tasks. For instance, current speed-oriented NAS approaches generally require days to accomplish one search trial on ImageNet, e.g., 8 _._ 3 GPU-days for ProxylessNAS (Cai et al., 2019) and 3 _._ 8 GPU-days for PC-DARTS (Xu et al., 2020). Therefore,we argue that it is essential to propose a new well-defined search space, which is not only expressive enough to cover the most powerful architectures, but also compact enough to filter out the poor architectures.

Motivated by Shu et al. (2020), who demonstrated that randomly replacing operators in a found architecture does not harm the accuracy much, we believe that it could not only bring no reduction to the testing accuracy but also significantly benefit the search efficiency if we omit the influence of operators and cluster architectures according to the topology. Thus, in this paper, we propose to

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

separately search for the network topology and the operators. We name this new method Effective NAS via First Topology Second Operator (FTSO).

In this paper, we mathematically prove that FTSO reduces the required parameters by 5 _._ 3 _×_ 10[7] and decreases the FLOPs per iteration by 1 _×_ 10[5] . Besides, FTSO significantly promotes the accuracy compared to the baseline by greatly shrinking the search space, reducing the operators complexity in magnitude and lowering the required searching period from 50 epochs to one iteration. What’s more, the Matthew effect is eased.

Furthermore, we empirically show that FTSO is superior in both efficiency and effectiveness, accomplishing the whole architecture search in 0 _._ 68 seconds. On ImageNet, FTSO can achieve 76 _._ 4% testing accuracy, 1 _._ 5% higher than the baseline, within merely 18 seconds. More importantly, when we only search for one iteration, FTSO can reach 75 _._ 64% testing accuracy, 0 _._ 74% higher than the baseline in just 0.68 seconds. Besides, if we allow FTSO to search for 19 minutes, 76 _._ 42% Top1 and 93 _._ 2% Top5 testing accuracy can be obtained. In addition, FTSO can reach 97 _._ 77% testing accuracy, 0 _._ 27% higher than the baseline, with 99 _._ 8% of search time saved on CIFAR10. Although in this paper we have only implemented FTSO within a continuous search space, we illustrate in Section 5 that FTSO can be seamlessly transferred to other NAS algorithms.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In general, existing NAS algorithms can be divided into three categories, namely, reinforcement learning-based, revolution-based and differentiable. Early-proposed reinforcement learning-based methods (Zoph & Le, 2017; Zoph et al., 2018) generally suffer from high computational cost and low-efficiency sampling. Instead of sampling a discrete architecture and then evaluating it, DARTS (Liu et al., 2019) treats the whole search space as a continuous super-net. It assigns every operator a real number weight and treats every node as the linear combination of all its transformed predecessors. To be specific, DARTS’s search space is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) containing two input nodes inherited from previous cells, four intermediate nodes and one output node. Each node denotes one latent representation and each edge denotes an operator. Every intermediate node **x** _j_ is calculated from all its predecessors **x** _i_ , i.e., **x** _j_ =[�] _i<j_ � _o∈O_ � _o[′]_ exp _∈O α_[exp] _[o] i,j[ α][o] i,j[′][o]_[(] **[x]** _[i]_[)][, where] _[ O]_ denotes the collection of all candidate operators, _αi,j[o]_[denotes the weight for operator] _[ o]_[ from node] _[ i]_ to _j_ . This strategy allows DARTS to directly use gradients to optimize the whole super-net. After the super-net converges, DARTS only retains the operators with the largest weights. In this way, the final discrete architecture is derived. The main defect of DARTS is that it needs to maintain and do all calculations on a giant super-net, which inevitably leads to heavy computational overheads and over-fitting.

Proposed to relieve the computational overhead of DARTS, DARTS-ES (Zela et al., 2020) reduces the number of searching epochs via early stopping, according to the Hessian matrix’s max eigenvalue. PC-DARTS (Xu et al., 2020) decreases the FLOPs per iteration by only calculating a proportion of the input channels and retaining the remainder unchanged, and normalizes the edge weights to stabilize the search. To be specific, in PC-DARTS, every intermediate node **x** _j_ is computed from all its predecessors **x** _i_ , i.e., **x** _j_ =[�] _i<j_ � _i[′]_ ex _<j_ p[exp] _βi,j[ β] i[′] ,j[f][i,j]_[(] **[x]** _[i]_[)][, where] _[ β][i,j]_[describes the input node] _i_ ’s importance to the node _j_ , and _fi,j_ is the weighted sum of all the candidate operators’ outputs _α[o]_ between node _i_ and _j_ . ## 3 FTSO: EFFECTIVE NAS VIA FIRST TOPOLOGY SECOND OPERATOR

Existing NAS approaches generally suffer from the heavy computational overhead and the unsatisfying testing accuracy leaded by the huge search space. Such problems are especially stern in one-shot and differentiable methods because, these algorithms need to maintain and even do all the calculations directly on the search space.
To ease such problems, it is of great demand to investigate the correlations among different architectures and to shrink the search space according to these prior knowledge. We notice that there

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

is an important observation in Shu et al. (2020) that randomly substituting the operators does not observably influence the testing accuracy in a found architecture. Therefore, it would be a great inspiration that we could cluster the architectures according to their connection topologies. To be specific, suppose we have found an architecture only containing the simplest operators achieves high accuracy on the testing set, if we replace all the skip connections in this architecture with powerful operators, the converted architecture can also perform well on the testing set with high confidence.

Figure 1: The main structure of FTSO.

Thus, in this paper, we first propose to find the effective network topology with simple operators and then we fix the topology, and search for the most suitable operators for the given topology. In this way, the testing accuracy can still be guaranteed, while the search space is shrunk in magnitude. We name this new NAS algorithm Effective NAS via First Topology Second Operator (FTSO).

As shown in Figure 1, we inherit the differentiable framework of PC-DARTS, and divide the architecture search into two phases. We name these two phases topology search and operator search, and illustrate how they work in Algorithm 1 and 2, respectively. In the first phase, we form a supernet only containing the simplest operator, _skip connection_ . Because the _skip connection_ operator contains no kernel weights, we only need to optimize the architecture parameters _βi,j_ , denoting the node _i_ ’s importance to the node _j_ . In fact, as shown in Table 3, the _max pooling_ operator also brings satisfying results for the topology search. There are two reasons why we use _skip connection_ . The first reason is that the _skip connection_ operator not only requires zero parameter, but also produces the minimum computational cost. The second reason is that max pooling may lead to the loss of useful information if the network is deep. Furthermore, as the the only difference between our topology search and the vanilla DARTS is the number of candidate operators, the pruned architecture’s connectivity can be guaranteed.

After the topology search, for every intermediate node _j_ , we only retain its connections to its predecessors _i[∗]_ with the highest two _βi,j_ similar to DARTS and PC-DARTS. In the second phase, we search for the operators suitable for the pruned topology with two strategies. One replaces each operator in the pruned topology with a mix-operator _fi,j_ , where _fi,j_ is the linear combination of all the candidate operators _o_ with weight _αi,j[o]_[,][denoting the importance of the operator] _[ o]_[ between the] node _i_ and _j_ . After that, we optimize the architecture parameters, _αi,j[o]_[,] _[ β][i,j]_[and the kernel weights] _ωi,j[o]_[alternatively. After the super-net converges, we only retain one operator] _[ o][∗]_[with the highest] _[ α] i,j[o]_ for every two connected nodes _i_ and _j_ . The other is to directly replace all the operators in the pruned topology with one single operator owning the highest model capacity, e.g., a convolution operator. In this paper, we take the second strategy as the default configuration because it is much more efficient and avoid over-fitting. The final architecture outputted by the first strategy is only a small part of the super-net. If the sub-graph can generalize perfectly on the testing set, the super-net must over-fit. Thus, under this circumstance, the optimum super-net on training set is not the optimum one on testing set, and so is the sub-graph. PC-DARTS has mentioned this issue and reduced the size of the super-net via only computing partial input channels. While in the second strategy, since no super-net is adopted and all the simple operators in the sub-graph are replaced with powerful

FTSO’s huge reduction on the parameter numbers provides us a large amount of benefits. As mentioned above, it allows the algorithm to converge in only a few iterations and prevent over-fitting. This is because when extracting the discrete sub-graph from the super-net, many architecture parameters are set to be 0. The introduced disturbance impacts more on the over-fitting super-nets since they prefer sharper local minimums. Furthermore, it avoids the Matthew’s effect. Each architecture has only one iteration to tune its kernel weights in DARTS. However, within one iteration, only the operators with few parameters can converge and thus, the simpler operators outperform the powerful ones in the super-net, then obtain larger gradients to enlarge their advantages. In this way, the found architectures tend to only contain the simplest operators and perform poorly on both training and testing set. Since FTSO only contains one operator with 0 parameter, the Matthew’s effect is eliminated.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

Our search algorithm is evaluated on the three most widely-used datasets in NAS papers, namely, CIFAR10(Krizhevsky et al., 2009) and ImageNet(Russakovsky et al., 2015) and NATS-Bench(Dong et al., 2020). Following DARTS, our search space contains a total eight candidate operators: 3 _×_ 3 and 5 _×_ 5 _separable convolutions_ , 3 _×_ 3 and 5 _×_ 5 _dilated separable convolutions_ , 3 _×_ 3 _max_ and _average pooling_ , _skip connection_ (i.e., _output_ = _input_ ) and _zero_ (i.e., _output_ = 0). When searching for the topology, we pick only one operator from the candidate set. As mentioned in Section 3, we have two strategies to determine the operators, including the one based on gradients and the one is directly replacing. In Section 4.1 and 4.2, we focus more on the second configuration. In Section

4.4, we have a comprehensive comparison on these two strategies. All detailed configurations are shown in Table 4 in Appendix A.1. In addition, most of our experiments only search for one epoch or one iteration because of the benefits of FTSO’s huge reduction on the parameter numbers. For more experimental supports, please refer to Section 4.4. Note that it is almost impossible for the existing models to obtain satisfying results via only searching for one epoch or one iteration because their super-nets contain a large amount of parameters, which require a long period to tune.

## 4.1 RESULTS ON CIFAR10

We compare FTSO to existing state-of-the-art NAS methods in Table 1. In the experiment, we only search for the topology with skip-connections and then replace them all with 3 _×_ 3 _separable convolutions_ . The reason that we do not adopt 5 _×_ 5 _separable convolutions_ is that the pre-processed input images do not have enough resolutions, and the network is rather deep. After a few layers, the convolution’s receptive field has been larger than the whole image. At that time, larger convolutional kernels may not bring benefits. Instead, the extra parameters brought by the larger kernel size may lead to over-fitting. On the other hand, suppose both the image’s resolution and the dataset’s scale are big enough and the evaluation period is adequate, the 5 _×_ 5 _separable convolution_ might be a better choice. The found architecture after one epoch’s search is shown in Figure 2. Because FTSO contains only a few trainable parameters, it can even achieve comparable accuracy to PCDARTS with only an one-time gradient update. Under this configuration, merely 0 _._ 68 seconds are required and 99 _._ 993% of the search time is saved. In addition, as shown in Table 3, when the topology is searched with powerful operators for a long period, additional operator search usually helps. However, when we search for the topology with simple operators for a short period, omitting the operator search may lead to better results. This is because with simple operators and very few updates, the found topology can already generalize quite well.

## 4.2 RESULTS ON IMAGENET

On ImageNet, we use similar configurations to those on CIFAR10. When searching, we have two configurations. The detailed configurations are shown in Appendix A.1. Our experiments in Table 1 show that FTSO is significantly superior to existing methods in both efficiency and effectiveness. The found architectures after one epoch’s search on CIFAR10 and the entire ImageNet are shown

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

in Figure 2 . It is surprising that the best architecture we found on ImageNet is the shallowest and widest one. Compared to the much more ”reasonable” architectures shown in Figure 2(e) and 2(f), which was found with the topology search only containing 3 _×_ 3 separable convolutions and an additional operator search on CIFAR10, the ”abnormal” architecture, containing the same amount of FLOPs and parameters, can achieve 0 _._ 78% higher testing accuracy. We think this is because the whole model is stacked with many cells. If the depth of each cell is too high, it leads to a very deep neural network. At that time, because all the operators in our found architecture are convolutions, we cannot use skip connections to facilitate gradients’ propagation in ResNet’s manner. In this way, both the vanishing and explosion of gradients may prevent the deeper models from higher performance.

## 4.3 RESULTS ON NATS-BENCH

In the search space of NATS-Bench, there are one input node, three intermediate nodes and one output node, and each intermediate node connects to all its predecessors. Here we implement FTSO based on DARTS instead of PC-DARTS, and we compare FTSO’s performance to other NAS algorithms in Table 2. It is shown that FTSO dominates DARTS on all configurations. Coinciding with our analysis in Section 3, the architectures found by DARTS tend to only contain simple operators, thus cannot achieve satisfying accuracy. For example, when searching on CIFAR10, the architecture found by DARTS is full of _skip connections_ as shown in Figure 2(h). By comparison, as shown in Figure 2(g), the architecture found by FTSO is much more powerful.

## 4.4 ABLATION STUDY

In terms of topology-only search, one epoch is just enough, thanks to the many fewer kernel weights contained in FTSO, and more search epochs bring obvious disadvantages because of over-fitting. Since one epoch performs better than more epochs, it raises the question whether one iteration is also superior to more iterations. In Figure 3(c) we show that although one iteration cannot surpass one epoch, it is better than a few iterations. This is because when we only search for one iteration, the model does over-fit the data, thus the model generalizes well. When we only searched for a few iterations, the number of different images seen by the model is not big enough. However, since the super-net only contains skip connections, such number of gradient updates has been enough for the architecture parameters to get over-fitted. This is the reason that a few iterations perform worse than one iteration. After we have searched for one whole epoch, the super-net has seen enormous different images, this helps it to generalize better on the testing set. This is the reason that one epoch performs the best. In terms of whether we should search for one iteration or two, in Figure 3(d), we show that both choices work well.

When we do not search for the operators after the topology search, we assign all the remaining edges a fixed operator. Thus, which operator we should choose becomes a critical question. Figure 3(e) show that 3 _×_ 3 _separable convolution_ can indeed outperform all other operators in terms of accuracy.

As shown in Figure 3(f), we find that under a different number of evaluation epochs, both the absolute value and the relative ranking of the same architecture’s testing accuracy may vary; i.e., some architectures performing well within 600 epochs perform poorly after 1200 epochs. However, in general, they still obey a positive correlation, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0 _._ 77, as shown in Figure 3(j). In terms of the generalization ability from CIFAR10 to ImageNet, Figure 3(l) reveals that the architectures performing well after long-term evaluation on CIFAR10 can usually generalize better on ImageNet, with the correlation coefficient of 0 _._ 7; yet, as shown in Figure 3(k), there is no guarantee that those working well on CIFAR10 within limited evaluation epochs can also dominate on ImageNet because this only proves that they can quickly converge, not that they can converge to a global optimum.

In Figure 3(g) and 3(h), we show that one epoch is not only an optimal choice on CIFAR10, but also enough for the topology-only search on CIFAR100. In addition, as the search epochs and iterations increase, the max eigenvalue of the loss’s Hessian matrix on the validation set increases and the testing accuracy is generally decreasing because the model’s generalization ability is dropping. ## 5 GENERALIZATION TO OTHER TASKS AND SEARCH SPACES

As shown in Section 4, FTSO works well under different search spaces and node numbers. Theoretically, FTSO’s advantages to DARTS can be enlarged while search space and node number increase. This is because FTSO reduces the computational cost from _O_ ( _n_[3] ) to _O_ ( _n_[2] ) and avoids over-fitting. Although in this paper, we establish FTSO within differentiable search spaces, in fact, the first topology second operator strategy is not limited within any specific search space or tasks. No matter the search space is discrete or continuous, we first shrink the candidate operator set, and only retain the simplest operator. After this, the size of the whole search space is reduced in magnitude. Then, we search for the best topology with whatever search algorithm. In this way, a promising topology can be found. Then, we can either directly assign each edge a powerful operator or use gradients to search for operators. Generally, the directly replacing strategy leads to higher accuracy, and the gradient-based strategy reduces model complexity.

## 6 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we propose an ultra computationally efficient neural architecture search method named FTSO, which reduces NAS’s search time cost from days to less than 0.68 seconds, while achieving 1.5% and 0.27% accuracy improvement on ImageNet and CIFAR10 respectively. Our key idea is to divide the search procedure into two sub-phases. In the first phase, we only search for the network’s topology with simple operators. Then, in the second phase, we fix the topology and only consider which operators we should choose.

Our strategy is concise in both theory and implementation, and our promising experimental results show that current NAS methods contain too much redundancy, which heavily impacts the efficiency and becomes a barrier to higher accuracy. What is more, our method is not bound by differentiable search spaces, it can also cooperate well with existing NAS approaches.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The proposed FTSO method presents a compelling and high-impact contribution to the field of Neural Architecture Search (NAS) by decoupling topology and operator search to achieve extreme efficiency. The manuscript is well-structured and positions the work effectively against current industry standards like PC-DARTS, supporting its claims with impressive (and potentially transformative) empirical results, such as reaching competitive accuracy on ImageNet within sub-second to sub-minute timeframes. However, the paper’s most significant challenge is the extreme disparity between its stated "0.68 seconds" search time and the standard expectations for NAS convergence; such a radical acceleration warrants a more rigorous justification to convince the reader that this is not an artifact of search-space over-simplification or overfitting. While the authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their two-stage approach, the clarity of the theoretical derivation—specifically the mathematical proof regarding the reduction in parameters and FLOPs—needs to be articulated with greater precision to validate that these gains are fundamentally justified by the search strategy rather than being dependent on specific, potentially narrow, experimental conditions.

While $|O|$ is a constant in static setups, the scaling behavior of DARTS with respect to the number of nodes is generally cited as $O(N^2)$. Claiming $O(N^3)$ seems to erroneously include the operator count as an $N$-dependent term or misrepresent the computational footprint of the super-net. This requires clarification to support the paper's central efficiency claim.

comments.title: Missing definition of tensor summation in Algorithms 1 and 2

It implies either the authors are only counting the final architecture's node-wise adds and ignoring the internal operations of the operators (which they claimed earlier were replaced by convolutions), or the formula is missing significant terms (like $C_{in} H_{out} W_{out} C_{out}$). This level of simplification is misleading as it ignores the dominant cost of the chosen operators (convolutions).

comments.title: Inconsistent Complexity Analysis: Order of Operations
comments.quote: Since n is usually close to p , FTSO reduces the number of operations from O(n^3) to O(n^2).
comments.explanation: The text states that the number of operations in DARTS is (n^2 - 3n)p, which is indeed O(n^2p). If n is close to p, this is O(n^3). However, the authors claim FTSO reduces this to O(n^2). The derivation for FTSO operations cites 2(n-3) for topology search and 2(n-3)p for operator search. Summing these yields O(np), not O(n^2), unless n and p are strictly linked. Furthermore, if n were 'close to p', then O(n^2) and O(p^2) would be equivalent, but the scaling behavior relative to search space expansion is inconsistently described.

comments.title: Dimensional Inconsistency in Complexity Calculation
comments.quote: 1/2(n(n-3)) CinHoutWoutCout + 1/2(pn(n-3)) HoutWoutCout = 1/2pn(n-3) HoutWoutCout(k^2 Cin + 1)
comments.explanation: The expression provided by the authors for DARTS FLOPs is dimensionally suspect. The first term 1/2(n(n-3)) is a count of edges; multiplied by (k^2 * Cin * Hout * Wout * Cout) represents convolutions. The second term 1/2(pn(n-3)) multiplied by (Hout * Wout * Cout) represents tensor summations. Summing these only factors correctly to (k^2 * Cin + 1) if the 'p' (number of operators) is distributed properly across the convolution term, which the algebra in the text fails to demonstrate clearly, suggesting a missing 'p' in the first term of the sum before factoring, or a miscalculation of the total operation count.

comments.title: Logical Flaw in "Matthew Effect" Mitigation Claim
comments.quote: Since FTSO only contains one operator with 0 parameter, the Matthew’s effect is eliminated.
comments.explanation: The authors define the 'Matthew effect' as simpler operators (with fewer parameters) converging faster and gaining larger gradients, thus dominating the super-net. If FTSO uses only skip connections (0 parameters) in the topology search phase, it essentially removes the competition between operators during that phase. While this avoids the gradient bias towards parameter-heavy operators *during* the first phase, it does not explain how the selection of the *topology* itself remains robust, nor why the subsequent operator search (which re-introduces operators) would be immune to the same effect if the 'Matthew' dynamic is inherent to gradient-based one-shot searching. The claim that the effect is 'eliminated' is a strong, potentially overreaching causal assertion.

comments.title: FLOPs calculation discrepancy
comments.quote: Specifically, a vanilla convolutional operator needs $k^2 C_{in}H_{out}W_{out}C_{out}$ FLOPs and $(k^2 C_{in} + 1) C_{out}$ parameters
comments.explanation: The formula for the number of parameters in a convolutional layer (with kernel size $k$ and input/output channels $C_{in}, C_{out}$) is typically $k^2 C_{in}C_{out} + C_{out}$ (weights plus bias). The provided formula $(k^2 C_{in} + 1) C_{out}$ is mathematically equivalent, which is correct. However, the FLOPs count $k^2 C_{in}H_{out}W_{out}C_{out}$ standardly represents the multiply-accumulate operations. The authors' later derivations in the text are internally consistent, but this section implies a simplification where bias is ignored in FLOPs but included in parameter counts. While acceptable as an approximation, the inconsistency in accounting for bias terms across different sections of the complexity analysis creates minor ambiguity for the reader.

comments.title: Implicit assumption of uniform channel dimensions
comments.quote: Thus, the total number of FLOPs and parameters of FTSO are $\frac{1}{2} n(n-3)H_{in}W_{in}C_{in}$ and $\frac{1}{2} n(n-3)$ respectively.
comments.explanation: The formula for FTSO parameters $\frac{1}{2} n(n-3)$ appears to assume that the number of parameters per edge is exactly 1. While the authors state that skip connections have 0 parameters (and they might be using 1 as a placeholder or to avoid division by zero in some implicit indexing), this contradicts the earlier parameter count of the candidate operators. Furthermore, the FLOPs expression $\frac{1}{2} n(n-3)H_{in}W_{in}C_{in}$ assumes skip connections involve operations proportional to the input feature map size. If a skip connection is a pure identity mapping, it should involve zero FLOPs. The math here suggests the authors are using a proxy value, which is not clearly defined, potentially misleading a reader trying to derive the exact reduction ratio.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding the Matthew effect
comments.quote: In this way, the found architectures tend to only contain the simplest operators and perform poorly on both training and testing set. Since FTSO only contains one operator with 0 parameter, the Matthew’s effect is eliminated.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that DARTS suffers from the Matthew effect, where simple operators 'obtain larger gradients to enlarge their advantages.' While it is a common critique that DARTS is biased toward skip connections, the claim that FTSO *eliminates* this effect by searching for topology first is an overclaim. By fixing the topology to skip connections during the first phase, they are not eliminating the bias; they are arguably baking it into the topology search. Whether this 'eliminates' or simply 'bypasses' the effect is a conceptual distinction that remains unsupported by the evidence provided.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance metrics in Table 2

It is highly improbable for a NAS method to yield identical accuracy across three drastically different evaluation target domains regardless of the search dataset. This suggests either a data transcription error in the paper or a methodological flaw where the search space or evaluation protocol is not varying as intended. This undermines the claim that FTSO is a robust NAS method across different search environments.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents FTSO, a two-stage NAS framework that decouples topology and operator search, making ambitious claims of reducing search time to under one second while outperforming baselines like PC-DARTS in accuracy. While the motivation to alleviate the computational burden of giant super-nets is relevant, the manuscript currently exhibits substantial deficiencies in clarity and presentation that obstruct a rigorous assessment. The most pressing issue is the severe degradation of mathematical notation; equations and numerical claims are rendered with artifacts (e.g., `$5 _._ 3 _×_ 10[7]$`, garbled gradient expressions in the Related Work), which undermines confidence in the reported parameter/FLOPs reductions and the technical soundness of the derivations. Additionally, the method description lacks precision, particularly regarding the "one iteration" search process, which is undefined in terms of optimization steps, convergence criteria, or heuristic rules, thereby casting doubt on the reproducibility and validity of the 0.68-second efficiency claim. The novelty of the topology-first, operator-second paradigm also needs sharper articulation relative to existing operator-agnostic or decoupled search methods, especially regarding the specific mechanism for mitigating the Matthew effect. Finally, the experimental claims require careful distinction between search efficiency and final model training cost, as well as verification of fair comparison protocols given the extreme speedup assertions.

comments.title: Mathematical & Complexity Derivation Errors
comments.quote: Since we have two input nodes, one output node and _n −_ 3 intermediate nodes, the super-net contains totally 1 2[(] _[n]_[2] _[−]_[3] _[n]_)( edges.
comments.explanation: The paper's mathematical derivations for computational complexity contain multiple interconnected errors. The edge count formula $1/2 n(n-3)$ (paragraph 24, 25) incorrectly computes the edges in the described DAG and introduces an unjustified factor of 1/2. The FLOPs derivation mistakenly uses input dimensions ($H_{in}W_{in}C_{in}$) instead of output dimensions ($H_{out}W_{out}C_{out}$) for tensor summations (paragraph 25, 26). The asymptotic claim of reducing operations from $O(n^3)$ to $O(n^2)$ conflates the number of architecture parameters with actual computational FLOPs, ignoring that DARTS already uses an $O(n^2)$ computational graph. Crucially, the stated 'total FLOPs and parameters of FTSO' only accounts for search-phase overhead (tensor additions with skip connections) rather than the final architecture after operator substitution, fundamentally misrepresenting model complexity. Finally, the numerical reduction ratio ($1.9 \times 10^{-8}$) is miscalculated, confusing a single-edge formula or absolute difference with a dimensionless ratio. These errors undermine the theoretical justification for FTSO's efficiency and require rigorous correction.

comments.title: Algorithm Pseudocode Contradictions & Underspecification
comments.quote: 6 Update weights _w_ by descending _\nabla wLtrain_ ( _w, \beta_ );
comments.explanation: The pseudocode in Algorithms 1 and 2 contains logical contradictions and underspecification that conflict with the text and standard NAS practices. Algorithm 1 states that skip connections contain zero learnable parameters, yet step 6 explicitly updates kernel weights $w$, which is logically invalid for the described topology search (paragraph 20, 21). The pruning logic is nested inside the `while still in the first epoch` loop (paragraph 21), implying dynamic mid-epoch edge removal, whereas the text states pruning occurs after the search concludes. Additionally, Algorithm 2's operator search uses a `while not converged` loop (paragraph 23), directly contradicting the paper's core claim of a highly efficient, single-iteration search. Algorithm 2 also conflates direct operator replacement with gradient-based mix-operators without clearly delineating the forward pass logic for each strategy. The pseudocode requires restructuring to accurately reflect the described procedure, early-stopping mechanism, and operator selection strategies.

comments.title: Overstated Claims Regarding the Matthew Effect
comments.quote: Each architecture has only one iteration to tune its kernel weights in DARTS. However, within one iteration, only the operators with few parameters can converge
comments.explanation: The justification for FTSO's handling of the Matthew effect relies on a misleading premise and uses overstrong language. The text claims DARTS only gets 'one iteration' to tune weights, which confuses the total search budget with the per-step gradient dynamics that cause simpler operators to dominate (paragraph 27). Consequently, claiming the Matthew effect is 'eliminated' is inaccurate; FTSO mitigates or bypasses it by decoupling topology search from operator selection and restricting the topology phase to a single operator type, not by altering fundamental optimization dynamics. The authors should clarify the distinction between search iteration limits and per-step convergence, and revise the claim to 'substantially mitigated' or 'bypassed' to align with established NAS literature.

comments.title: Inconsistencies in Reported Experimental Results & Claims
comments.quote: merely 0 _._ 68 seconds are required
comments.explanation: Multiple empirical claims across the paper contain numerical discrepancies and contradictions. The reported search time (0.68s in text vs. ~17.3s derived from Table 1's GPU-days) and time-saving percentages (99.8% vs. 99.993%) do not align (paragraph 32). The absolute parameter reduction (~$5.3 \times 10^7$) conflicts with the derived relative ratio ($1.9 \times 10^{-8}$), and the discrepancy cannot be reconciled without specifying the number of network cells (paragraph 8). Furthermore, the text explicitly asserts that extending topology search beyond one epoch degrades performance, but Table 3 ablation results show that 50 epochs of topology-only search actually yield lower error rates (paragraph 46). These inconsistencies need to be resolved to ensure transparency, reproducibility, and accurate reporting of the method's efficiency and performance.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents FTSO, a two-stage NAS framework that decouples topology and operator search, making ambitious claims of reducing search time to under one second while outperforming baselines like PC-DARTS in accuracy. comments.title: Complexity justification during search phase is inaccurate
comments.quote: This is because in the topology search, every edge contains only one operator; and in the topology search, every intermediate node only connects to two predecessors.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that intermediate nodes connect to only two predecessors during the topology search to justify the reduced $O(n^2)$ computational complexity. However, this contradicts the standard differentiable NAS procedure (and the PC-DARTS framework they explicitly inherit), where a fully connected super-net is used throughout the optimization of architecture parameters. As shown in Algorithm 1, edge pruning to retain only the top two predecessors occurs *after* the search loop concludes (lines 7–11), not during the search itself. Consequently, forward and backward passes during the topology search still operate on a dense graph, making the stated complexity justification for the search phase technically inaccurate. The reduced cost only applies to the final pruned architecture, not the search process itself.

comments.title: Incorrect edge count formula for the super-net
comments.quote: Since we have two input nodes, one output node and _n −_ 3 intermediate nodes, the super-net contains totally 1 2[(] _[n]_[2] _[−]_[3] _[n]_[)][ edges.
comments.explanation: The formula $\frac{1}{2}n(n-3)$ does not correctly compute the number of edges in the described directed acyclic graph. For a NAS cell with $n$ total nodes (2 inputs, 1 output, $n-3$ intermediate nodes) where directed edges exist from $i$ to $j$ for $i<j$, the total number of edges is $n(n-1)/2$. The authors' formula undercounts the edges significantly. While this appears to be a typographical error, it propagates to the subsequent FLOPs and parameter derivations in Section 3, requiring correction for mathematical rigor.

comments.title: Contradiction regarding skip connection parameters in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Assign each edge _ei,j_ a _skip connection_ operator _oi,j_ with kernel weights _wi,j_ ;
comments.explanation: The main text explicitly states that 'the _skip connection_ operator contains no kernel weights, we only need to optimize the architecture parameters _βi,j_.' However, Algorithm 1 step 2 explicitly assigns kernel weights $w_{i,j}$ to the skip connection operator. This is a direct contradiction, as skip connections are identity mappings and should carry zero learnable parameters. The algorithm pseudocode or the accompanying text needs to be corrected to reflect that no kernel weights are assigned or updated for skip connections.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding DARTS optimization dynamics and Matthew's effect
comments.quote: Each architecture has only one iteration to tune its kernel weights in DARTS. However, within one iteration, only the operators with few parameters can converge
comments.explanation: The statement 'Each architecture has only one iteration to tune its kernel weights in DARTS' is highly misleading. In DARTS, kernel weights are updated over many epochs/iterations. The authors' intent is to explain that within a *single* optimization step (or epoch) of parameter sharing, simple operations (like skip connections) with fewer parameters converge or exhibit stronger gradients faster, skewing the architecture weights toward them. The current phrasing incorrectly suggests that DARTS as a whole only runs for one iteration, confusing the total search budget with the per-step dynamics that cause the Matthew effect. Clarification is needed to distinguish between the total search duration and the gradient imbalance mechanism.

comments.title: Unjustified asymptotic complexity claim
comments.quote: FTSO reduces the number of operations from _O_ ( _n_[3] ) to _O_ ( _n_[2] ).
comments.explanation: The authors derive the number of edges in the super-net as ½(n²-3n), which is inherently O(n²). Unless the number of candidate operators p or the network depth scales linearly with n, the computational operations per epoch should also scale as O(n²). Jumping to O(n³) for DARTS without specifying how p or depth contributes to a cubic term is mathematically inconsistent with the preceding derivation. The claim undermines the theoretical justification for FTSO's efficiency gains.

comments.title: Disconnect between absolute and relative parameter/FLOP reductions
comments.quote: FTSO reduces the required parameters by 5 _._ 3 _×_ 10[7] ... requires only ... 1 _._ 9 _×_ 10[−8] times the parameters ... compared to those of DARTS.
comments.explanation: The introduction claims an absolute reduction of ~5.3×10⁷ parameters, while Section 3 claims a relative reduction factor of ~1.9×10⁻⁸ based on a single cell configuration (n=7, p=8). These figures are not reconciled. If the relative ratio is truly ~10⁻⁸, the absolute reduction would depend entirely on the total number of cells in the searched network, which is never specified. The numbers appear to contradict or originate from unexplained experimental setups, making it impossible to verify the claimed efficiency gains.

comments.title: Discrepancy between reported search time and table values
comments.quote: merely 0 _._ 68 seconds are required
comments.explanation: The text in Section 4.1 states the search takes 0.68 seconds. However, Table 1 lists the Search Cost for FTSO (CIFAR10 + 1 iteration) as 2×10⁻⁴ GPU-days, which equals approximately 17.3 seconds. This is a ~25× discrepancy. Additionally, the text claims 99.8% time saved, but if the baseline PC-DARTS takes 0.1 GPU-days (8,640s), saving 0.68s yields ~99.99% savings. The numerical claims do not align with each other or standard time conversions.

comments.title: Algorithm termination condition contradicts core 'one iteration' claim
comments.quote: 5 while not converged do
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction heavily emphasize FTSO's speed, explicitly stating it searches for 'one iteration' or '0.68 seconds'. However, Algorithm 2's operator search phase uses a standard convergence loop (`while not converged do`). The pseudocode does not cap iterations at 1 or include the early-stopping mechanism referenced in the text. This leaves the exact procedure underspecified and potentially contradictory, as a reader following the algorithm might run multiple iterations, defeating the paper's main efficiency claim.

comments.title: Overstated claim on Matthew effect elimination
comments.quote: Since FTSO only contains one operator with 0 parameter, the Matthew’s effect is eliminated.
comments.explanation: While decoupling topology and operators significantly mitigates the Matthew effect by ensuring operators receive more balanced gradient updates per epoch, claiming it is completely 'eliminated' is too strong. The effect can still re-emerge during the operator search phase if validation loss optimization favors simpler architectures, or due to dataset biases. 'Mitigated' or 'substantially reduced' would be more accurate and scientifically defensible.

comments.title: Inconsistent tensor dimensions in FLOP calculation
comments.quote: Because each tensor summation consumes $H_{in}W_{in}C_{in}$ FLOPs, DARTS requires totally[1] 2 _[pk]_[2] _[n]_[(] _[n][−]_ 3) _CinHoutWoutCout_ +[1] 2 _[pn]_[(] _[n][−]_[3)] _[H][out][W][out][C][out]_[=][1] 2 _[pn]_[(] _[n][−]_[3)] _[H][out][W][out][C][out]_[(] _[k]_[2] _[C][in]_[+1)][ FLOPs] ... Thus, the total number of FLOPs and parameters of FTSO are[1] 2 _[n]_[(] _[n][ −]_[3)] _[H][in][W][in][C][in]_[and] 1 2 _[n]_[(] _[n][−]_[3)][respectively.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note that aggregating transformed inputs involves tensor addition. However, they state that each summation consumes $H_{in}W_{in}C_{in}$ FLOPs, referring to the input tensor's dimensions. In a cell, node $j$ sums the outputs of its predecessors, so the summation cost must scale with the activation size at node $j$, i.e., $H_{out}W_{out}C_{out}$. This typo propagates to the final FLOP count for FTSO. While it does not change the asymptotic scaling argument, it is a notable notation error that should be corrected for mathematical rigor and reproducibility.

comments.title: Erroneous numerical claim for parameter reduction ratio
comments.quote: Then, our algorithm requires only _p_ ( _k_[2] _Cin_ 1+1) _Cout_[=][1] _[.]_[9] _[ ×]_[ 10] _[−]_[8][times the parameters and] _p_ ( _k_[2] _C_ 1 _in_ +1)[= 9] _[.]_[8] _[ ×]_[ 10] _[−]_[6][times the forward-propagation FLOPs per iteration compared to those of] DARTS.
comments.explanation: The authors claim FTSO requires $1.9 \times 10^{-8}$ times the parameters of DARTS. The provided expression $p(k^2C_{in}+1)C_{out}$ evaluates to $\approx 5.25 \times 10^7$ with the stated hyperparameters, not a dimensionless ratio near $10^{-8}$. Logically, comparing a super-net with $p$ candidate operators per edge to a final architecture with 1 operator per edge yields a parameter ratio of exactly $1/p = 0.125$. If comparing the topology search phase (which uses only skip connections with 0 parameters), the ratio would be 0. The value $1.9 \times 10^{-8}$ appears to be a miscalculation or garbled artifact, possibly confusing the parameter ratio with the FLOPs ratio or applying an incorrect denominator. This undermines the specific quantitative claim about parameter efficiency, though the qualitative message of drastic reduction remains valid.

comments.title: Ambiguous conflation of parameter space size and computational operations
comments.quote: Since _n_ is usually close to _p_ , FTSO reduces the number of operations from _O_ ( _n_[3] ) to _O_ ( _n_[2] ).
comments.explanation: The text claims FTSO reduces 'operations' from $O(n^3)$ to $O(n^2)$. Given the preceding context ($\frac{1}{2}n(n-3)p$ operators in DARTS vs $\frac{1}{2}n(n-3)$ in FTSO topology search), this refers to the number of architecture parameters in the continuous relaxation, not actual FLOPs or computational graph operations. DARTS already operates on an $O(n^2)$ computational graph due to weight sharing. A careful reader might misinterpret 'operations' as compute complexity rather than search space/parameter size. Clarifying that this refers to the number of learnable architecture weights would prevent ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistent search time saving percentages across sections
comments.quote: Abstract: ...with 99.8% of search time saved on CIFAR10. ... Section 4.1: ...merely 0.68 seconds are required and 99.993% of the search time is saved.
comments.explanation: The abstract reports a 99.8% time saving on CIFAR10, while Section 4.1 reports 99.993%. Both likely compare FTSO (1 iteration/epoch) against a multi-epoch baseline, but the discrepancy suggests inconsistent baseline runtimes or rounding practices. While the core claim of massive speedup is unaffected, unifying the reported figures would improve precision and reader trust.

comments.title: Ambiguous Parameter/FLOP Counting Conflates Search Overhead with Model Complexity
comments.quote: only1/2 _[n]_[(] _[n][ −]_[3)] _[tensor][summation][need][to][be][calcu-] lated. Thus, the total number of FLOPs and parameters of FTSO are1/2 _[n]_[(] _[n][ −]_[3)] _[H][in][W][in][C][in]_[and] 1/2 _[n]_[(] _[n][−]_[3)][respectively.]
comments.explanation: I initially noted that the authors compare DARTS's super-net parameter count (which maintains $p$ operators per edge simultaneously) against FTSO's count, which only sums tensor additions. The summary explicitly lists 'FTSO parameters per iteration: 1/2 n(n-3)', matching the text. However, this counts only the computational overhead of the search phase (where skip connections are used, hence 0 kernel parameters/FLOPs), completely ignoring the parameters and FLOPs of the actual convolutions that replace them in the final architecture or operator search phase. While this accurately reflects *search cost*, labeling it as the 'total number of FLOPs and parameters of FTSO' misleads readers into thinking the final model's complexity is this low. This is a definition ambiguity that blurs the line between algorithmic efficiency and model capacity.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 Pruning Placement Contradicts Text and Standard Practice
comments.quote: while _still in the first epoch_ **do** **4** Forward-propagate following _nj_ =[] _i<j[o]_[(] _[n][i]_[)] _[β][i,j]_[;] **5** Update architecture _β_ by descending _∇βLval_ ( _w, β_ ); **6** Update weights _w_ by descending _∇wLtrain_ ( _w, β_ ); **7 foreach** _node nj ∈ Ap_ **do 8** _Tj ←_ the second largest _βi,j_ ; **9 foreach** _node ni_ **do 10 if** _βi,j < Tj_ **then 11** Prune edge _ei,j_
comments.explanation: Reading Algorithm 1, I see the pruning logic (lines 8-11) nested inside the `while still in the first epoch` loop. The text explicitly states: 'After the topology search, for every intermediate node j, we only retain its connections to its predecessors i with the highest two βij'. Dynamically pruning edges mid-epoch would repeatedly alter the graph topology during gradient updates, which is non-standard and likely destabilizes the differentiable relaxation. The intent is clearly to optimize the topology weights first, then prune once at the end of the epoch. The algorithm's indentation and loop structure are underspecified and misleading.

comments.title: Questionable Justification for Matthew Effect Avoidance
comments.quote: Each architecture has only one iteration to tune its kernel weights in DARTS. However, within one iteration, only the operators with few parameters can converge and thus, the simpler operators outperform the powerful ones in the super-net, then obtain larger gradients to enlarge their advantages.
comments.explanation: The authors claim DARTS only gets one iteration to tune weights, and that this short horizon causes parameter-light operators (skip connections) to dominate due to faster convergence. However, standard DARTS protocols train for ~50 epochs, and the Matthew effect (skip connection dominance) is well-documented even after full training because of gradient imbalance and architectural bias, not merely a lack of optimization steps. By anchoring their justification to a 'one iteration' claim without clarifying this refers to a specific ablation or misstates DARTS training, the theoretical reasoning for why FTSO avoids the effect becomes insufficient. FTSO avoids the effect primarily because skip connections are decoupled into the topology phase and replaced entirely in the operator phase, not because of iteration limits.

comments.title: Numerical Ratio vs. Absolute Difference Calculation Error
comments.quote: Then, our algorithm requires only _p_ ( _k_[2] _Cin_ 1+1) _Cout_[=][1] _[.]_[9] _[ ×]_[ 10] _[−]_[8][times the parameters and] _p_ ( _k_[2] _C_ 1 _in_ +1)[= 9] _[.]_[8] _[ ×]_[ 10] _[−]_[6][times the forward-propagation FLOPs per iteration compared to those of] DARTS.
comments.explanation: The text states the algorithm requires 'X times the parameters' compared to DARTS, but the computed value `p(k^2 C_in+1)C_out` with the given configuration ($p=8, k=5, C_{in}=C_{out}=512$) yields approximately $5.25 \times 10^7$. This is an absolute parameter count difference, not a multiplicative ratio. The OCR artifacts suggest they wrote $1.9 \times 10^{-8}$ times, but numerically the phrasing 'times the parameters' combined with a large absolute number creates a contradiction. It is unclear whether they meant a ratio of $\approx 1.9 \times 10^{-8}$ relative to DARTS's super-net size, or if they mistakenly listed the absolute difference as the ratio. This numerical inconsistency undermines the precision of the claimed efficiency gains.

comments.title: Inconsistent edge count formula in complexity derivation
comments.quote: Since DARTS has $1/2 p n (n-3)$ edges, it needs to compute $1/2 p n (n-3)$ convolutions and $1/2 p n (n-3)$ tensor summations.
comments.explanation: Earlier in Section 3, the authors correctly derive the number of edges in a DARTS super-net as $2\binom{n}{2} - 3n = n(n-3)$. However, when calculating computational cost, they consistently introduce an unjustified factor of $1/2$, writing $1/2 p n (n-3)$ edges. This factor of $1/2$ propagates through the FLOPs and parameter formulas, casting doubt on the numerical accuracy of the complexity reduction claims. If $n(n-3)$ is indeed the correct edge count, the stated costs are halved, which does not change the asymptotic complexity but makes the concrete numerical comparisons (e.g., $1.9 \times 10^{-8}$ ratio) mathematically inconsistent with their own definitions.

comments.title: Notation mismatch in FLOPs derivation (input vs. output dimensions)
comments.quote: Thus, the total number of FLOPs and parameters of FTSO are $1/2 n(n-3) H_{in} W_{in} C_{in}$ and $1/2 n(n-3)$ respectively.
comments.explanation: The authors note that FTSO topology search uses skip connections (0 FLOPs), so the only remaining computation is tensor summation across edges. Tensor addition operations are performed on the feature maps produced by the preceding nodes. Therefore, the FLOPs should scale with the output feature map dimensions ($H_{out} W_{out} C_{out}$), not the input dimensions ($H_{in} W_{in} C_{in}$). Using $H_{in}$ here is inconsistent with the DARTS FLOPs formula earlier in the same paragraph (which correctly uses $H_{out} W_{out} C_{out}$) and with standard deep learning complexity analysis. It is unclear whether this is a typo or an unexplained assumption that input and output tensor sizes are identical at every layer.

comments.title: Direct contradiction between textual claims and Table 3 results
comments.quote: One epoch of topology search is optimal; additional epochs induce overfitting and degrade performance.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that extending topology search beyond one epoch degrades performance. However, Table 3 shows that `CIF. Topo(skip,50ep.)` achieves a CIFAR10 error of 2.48%, which is strictly better than `CIF. Topo(skip,1ep.)` at 2.54%. While the authors may be referring specifically to the *combined* search (topology + operator), the blanket statement in the text is contradicted by the ablation results in the table. This creates ambiguity about whether 1 epoch is truly optimal for topology-only search or if longer searches are beneficial when operator search is omitted.

comments.title: Overstated causal claim regarding the Matthew effect
comments.quote: Since FTSO only contains one operator with 0 parameter, the Matthew’s effect is eliminated.
comments.explanation: The Matthew effect in NAS refers to the bias where simpler operators (like skip connections) dominate the search because they have fewer parameters and converge faster. The authors claim this effect is 'eliminated' by FTSO. Conceptually, the effect isn't eliminated; rather, the competitive landscape is artificially removed by restricting the topology search to a single operator type. There is no longer a competing 'complex' operator to suffer from slower convergence, so the dynamic that causes the Matthew effect simply cannot manifest. This phrasing slightly overstates the mechanism and could mislead readers into thinking FTSO actively counteracts a known optimization bias rather than bypassing it through search space restriction.

comments.title: Underspecified algorithm branching in operator search
comments.quote: Algorithm 2 operator search / 1 if replace with convolutions then 2 Replace all the retained operators oi,j in Ap with convolutions; / 3 else exp α[o] i,j / 4 Assign each node nj ← ∑ i<j ∑ o∈O exp[ α[o] i,j] o(ni);
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 attempts to describe two distinct strategies (direct replacement vs. gradient-based mix-operator) but structures them in a way that obscures the forward pass logic. Step 1-2 handles direct replacement, but Step 4 immediately redefines node aggregation using a mix-operator formula inside an `else` block. It is unclear whether Step 4 is meant to describe the validation forward pass for the gradient strategy, or if it incorrectly implies that the node values are being reassigned during inference. A clear separation of the two strategies into distinct procedural blocks or explicit forward/backward pass annotations would be necessary for faithful reproduction.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 updates kernel weights during zero-parameter topology search
comments.quote: **6** Update weights _w_ by descending _∇wLtrain_ ( _w, β_ );
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by why the topology search algorithm includes a step to update kernel weights $w$. Upon cross-referencing with the text, the authors explicitly state: 'Because the _skip connection_ operator contains no kernel weights, we only need to optimize the architecture parameters _βi,j_'. Since skip connections have zero learnable parameters, computing and applying gradients for $w$ is computationally invalid and logically contradictory. This step introduces an unnecessary and erroneous backward pass that contradicts the method's core efficiency premise and would mislead implementers attempting to reproduce the topology phase.

comments.title: FLOPs and parameters calculation contradicts the direct substitution strategy
comments.quote: While in FTSO, if we first search for the topology and then directly substituting the operators, only[1] 2 _[n]_[(] _[n][ −]_[3)][tensor][summation][need][to][be][calcu-] lated. Thus, the total number of FLOPs and parameters of FTSO are[1] 2 _[n]_[(] _[n][ −]_[3)] _[H][in][W][in][C][in]_[and] 1 2 _[n]_[(] _[n][−]_[3)][respectively.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that their 'direct substitution' strategy replaces topology edges with high-capacity operators (e.g., convolutions) to 'promote model capacity'. Yet, they simultaneously claim this substitution requires only tensor summations, deriving FLOPs and parameters based purely on summation costs ($ rac{1}{2}n(n-3)H_{in}W_{in}C_{in}$ and $ rac{1}{2}n(n-3)$). This is a fundamental logical inconsistency. Convolutional operators inherently require convolution FLOPs and learnable parameters, not just tensor additions. The calculated costs actually correspond to the *topology search phase* (which uses skip connections), not the final architecture after operator substitution. Conflating search-phase complexity with final model complexity severely undermines their efficiency claims and misrepresents the deployed architecture's actual computational footprint.

comments.title: Numerical reduction ratio incorrectly uses a single-edge operator formula
comments.quote: our algorithm requires only p ( k [2] Cin 1+1) Cout [=1.9 × 10^-8 times the parameters and p ( k [2] C 1 in +1)[= 9.8 × 10^-6 times the forward-propagation FLOPs per iteration compared to those of] DARTS.
comments.explanation: The preceding paragraph correctly establishes that FTSO's total parameters are $ rac{1}{2}n(n-3)$. However, the numerical comparison ratio is computed using $p(k^2 C_{in} + 1)C_{out}$, which is the parameter count for a *single* operator/edge, not the entire FTSO architecture. Using a single-edge metric to represent the whole model's cost is a derivation error. This invalidates the claimed $1.9 	imes 10^{-8}$ parameter reduction factor and misleads readers about the true magnitude of FTSO's efficiency gains compared to DARTS. The authors likely intended to use the full FTSO parameter count in the numerator but accidentally inserted the single-operator formula, breaking the mathematical comparison.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
As an academic reviewer, I would offer the following high-level feedback:

The paper presents an intriguing and ambitious premise—decoupling topology and operator search in NAS to achieve extreme efficiency—but the current draft suffers from several critical issues that undermine its credibility and clarity. First, the claims of achieving full architecture search in "0.68 seconds" and "18 seconds on ImageNet" are extraordinary and warrant substantially more rigorous validation; the paper lacks sufficient detail on experimental setup, hardware specifications, and reproducibility, and such dramatic speed improvements raise concerns about whether the search process is genuinely exploring the space or merely exploiting trivial solutions. Second, the theoretical claims of parameter reduction by \(5.3 \times 10^7\) and FLOPs reduction by \(1 \times 10^5\) are presented without derivations or clear context, making them seem arbitrary rather than grounded in the proposed architecture. Third, the writing suffers from imprecision and overstatement (e.g., "significantly promotes the accuracy," "Matthew effect is eased"), and the introduction conflates motivational insights from prior work, such as Shu et al. (2020), with the paper's own contributions without clearly delineating the novel technical advancement. The most significant issue, however, is the lack of clarity on how the two-stage search is operationalized—specifically, how topology and operators are separated, what search space is used for each stage, and how the results are combined—without which the core methodology remains opaque and the impressive empirical claims unverifiable. The paper would benefit greatly from a more measured tone, detailed algorithmic exposition, and thorough ablation studies to substantiate its remarkable efficiency and accuracy results.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about search time in abstract
comments.quote: FTSO not only reduces NAS’s search time from days to 0.68 seconds
comments.explanation: The abstract states that FTSO reduces search time 'from days to 0.68 seconds' without specifying the dataset. Later in the abstract and introduction, the paper reports that on ImageNet the search takes 18 seconds (for 76.4% accuracy) and that a one-iteration search on ImageNet takes 0.68 seconds (yielding 75.64% accuracy). A reader could reasonably interpret the opening sentence as meaning the total search time on any dataset (including ImageNet) is 0.68 seconds, which is inconsistent with the later statement that 'within merely 18 seconds' FTSO achieves 76.4% on ImageNet. The claim is likely intended to refer to a specific setting (e.g., one iteration on CIFAR10), but as written it is ambiguous and could mislead.

comments.title: Typo: 'revolution-based' should be 'evolution-based'
comments.quote: existing NAS algorithms can be divided into three categories, namely, reinforcement learning-based, revolution-based and differentiable.
comments.explanation: The term 'revolution-based' is not standard in the NAS literature; the intended category is almost certainly 'evolution-based' (i.e., evolutionary algorithms). This is a clear typo that could confuse readers unfamiliar with the field. The context does not resolve it because no other mention of 'revolution' occurs, and it is a straightforward substitution error likely introduced during OCR or transcription.

comments.title: Errors in parameter and FLOP reduction claims
comments.quote: we mathematically prove that FTSO reduces the required parameters by 5.3 × 10^7 and decreases the FLOPs per iteration by 1 × 10^5 .
comments.explanation: The paper contains two separate errors regarding parameter and FLOP reductions. First, the expression for the ratio (p*(k^2*Cin+1)*Cout = 1.9e-8 times the parameters) omits the reciprocal; the correct expression should be 1/(p*(...)) to yield the tiny number. Second, the claimed absolute reduction of 5.3e7 parameters does not match the computed reduction of roughly 7.34e8 from the given values (k=5, Cin=Cout=512, n=7, p=8). These inconsistencies undermine the quantitative claims.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 assigns kernel weights to skip connection
comments.quote: Assign each edge e_{i,j} a skip connection operator o_{i,j} with kernel weights w_{i,j} ;
comments.explanation: A skip connection is an identity mapping with no learnable parameters. Giving it kernel weights w_{i,j} contradicts the earlier statement that it contains zero parameters and is conceptually wrong. The pseudocode should either omit the kernel weights or assign them as empty (zero parameters). This is a minor inconsistency that could confuse a reader implementing the algorithm.

comments.title: Missing β update in operator search algorithm
comments.quote: After that, we optimize the architecture parameters, α_{i,j}^o, β_{i,j} and the kernel weights w_{i,j}^o alternatively.
comments.explanation: The prose states that in the gradient-based operator search (first strategy) the architecture parameters β are also optimized. However, Algorithm 2 (operator search) only shows updates for α and w (steps 6-7) and makes no mention of β. Since the topology is already pruned and fixed, it is unclear whether β should be re-optimized or frozen. The algorithm as written is incomplete and inconsistent with the text, leaving the reader unsure how β is handled.

comments.title: Misleading claim about O(n^3) complexity for DARTS
comments.quote: Since n is usually close to p, FTSO reduces the number of operations from O(n^3) to O(n^2).
comments.explanation: The number of operations in DARTS is (n^2-3n)p/2, which is O(n^2 p). Since p is a constant (the number of candidate operators, e.g., 8) and does not scale with n, the asymptotic complexity is O(n^2), not O(n^3). The authors justify O(n^3) by stating that n is usually close to p, but p is fixed independent of n in practice; for typical n=7, p=8, the numerical values are close but asymptotically p is constant. Therefore, the claim that FTSO reduces complexity from O(n^3) to O(n^2) is not accurate under standard asymptotic analysis and overstates the improvement.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of DARTS training iterations for operator weights
comments.quote: Each architecture has only one iteration to tune its kernel weights in DARTS.
comments.explanation: This statement is misleading. In DARTS, the super-net is trained over many iterations (typically 50 epochs), and during each iteration the kernel weights of all operators are updated via gradient descent. The phrase 'only one iteration' suggests that each operator's weights are updated only once, which is not true. The authors seem to confuse the discrete architecture search with the super-net training process. This mischaracterization undermines the explanation of the Matthew effect.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to topology search in operator description
comments.quote: This is because in the topology search, every edge contains only one operator; and in the topology search, every intermediate node only connects to two predecessors.
comments.explanation: The second clause should refer to the operator search phase, not the topology search. In topology search, every intermediate node can connect to all predecessors (fully connected DAG), while after pruning during operator search, each intermediate node retains exactly two predecessors. The repeated 'in the topology search' causes confusion about which phase is being described. This is a minor typographical/editing error.

comments.title: Identical accuracy values for FTSO across different search datasets in Table 2
comments.explanation: In Table 2, the FTSO row shows the exact same three testing accuracies (93.98% on CIFAR10, 70.22% on CIFAR100, 45.57% on ImageNet) for each of the three search-dataset columns (Search on CIFAR10, Search on CIFAR100, Search on ImageNet). This is highly implausible because the search dataset influences the discovered architecture and thus its transfer performance. The surrounding DARTS rows show variation across search datasets, as expected. The identical numbers suggest a data-entry error, a copy-paste mistake, or that the authors inadvertently reported a single set of results in all three columns. Without explanation, this undermines the credibility of the comparison with DARTS. The context (the text after the table) does not resolve the issue—it only states that FTSO dominates DARTS, but does not comment on the repetition.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the phrase 'the second largest β_{i,j}' in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: T_j ← the second largest β_{i,j} ; ... if β_{i,j} < T_j then Prune edge e_{i,j}
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1 (topology search), the condition for pruning retains only the two edges with the largest β values for each intermediate node j. The algorithm defines T_j as the second largest β_{i,j}, then prunes any edge with β_{i,j} < T_j. However, the pseudocode does not specify whether T_j is computed over all i < j (i.e., all possible predecessor nodes) or only over nodes that currently have edges (which are all pairs initially). The phrase 'the second largest β_{i,j}' is ambiguous because it does not clarify whether connections with β = 0 (or 'zero' operator) are considered. If a node has multiple predecessors with equal β values, the tie-breaking rule is not stated. This could lead to an implementation where the number of retained edges is not exactly two per intermediate node. The context from Section 3 clarifies that 'for every intermediate node j, we only retain its connections to its predecessors i* with the highest two β_{i,j} similar to DARTS and PC-DARTS', which resolves the ambiguity: the intended behavior is to keep exactly the top two β values, and if there are ties, the algorithm would need a rule. The pseudocode is underspecified but the prose provides the intended interpretation. This is a minor underspecification in the algorithm description.

comments.title: Questionable claim about 'one iteration' being sufficient and outperforming 'a few iterations'
comments.quote: One iteration for topology search, while worse than one epoch, is better than a few iterations because the model overfits the data, while with few iterations the model sees too few images.
comments.explanation: This claim from the summary (not the passage itself) is flagged for completeness. The passage does not contain this quote directly, but it appears in the summary. The logic is puzzling: 'one iteration' sees even fewer images than 'a few iterations', so the justification that 'with few iterations the model sees too few images' would apply even more strongly to one iteration. The claim seems to rely on an overfitting argument where after more iterations the model overfits, but for one iteration it does not. However, if one iteration sees very few images, it might not converge at all, while a few iterations might yield a better trade-off. The passage under review discusses the observation that 'as the search epochs and iterations increase, the max eigenvalue of the loss’s Hessian matrix on the validation set increases and the testing accuracy is generally decreasing because the model’s generalization ability is dropping.' This supports the general trend that longer search hurts, but it does not specifically argue that 'one iteration' beats 'a few iterations.' The authors' intent is to show that very short search (1 iteration) avoids overfitting that sets in after a few iterations. However, the reasoning that 'few iterations see too few images' while one iteration sees even fewer is internally inconsistent. This is a moderate issue because it is a key claim about the optimal search duration, and the justification is logically flawed. However, the empirical results in Table 3 (e.g., CIF. Topo(skip,1it.) vs CIF. Topo(skip,1ep.)) show that 1 iteration is worse than 1 epoch but still competitive, so the claim might be hyperbolic rather than strictly wrong. I will flag it as questionable.
